name: Schema Migration with psql and Version Control
description: Manage database schema changes safely using numbered migration files, psql, and version control for reproducible deployments.

steps.title: Create a migrations tracking table
steps.description: Set up a table to track which migrations have been applied.
steps.command: psql -U postgres -d mydb -c "CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS schema_migrations (version VARCHAR(14) PRIMARY KEY, applied_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(), description TEXT);"

steps.title: Create a numbered migration file
steps.description: Write a SQL migration file with a timestamp-based version number.
steps.tip: Always use timestamps (YYYYMMDDHHMMSS) as version prefixes to avoid ordering conflicts between team members.

steps.title: Apply a migration within a transaction
steps.description: Execute the migration inside a transaction so it rolls back entirely on failure.
steps.warning: Some DDL statements (like CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY) cannot run inside a transaction. Handle those separately.

steps.title: Record the migration as applied
steps.description: Insert a row into the tracking table after a successful migration.
steps.command: psql -U postgres -d mydb -c "INSERT INTO schema_migrations (version, description) VALUES ('20260311120000', 'create_users');"

steps.title: Check migration status
steps.description: List all applied migrations and compare with migration files on disk.
steps.command: psql -U postgres -d mydb -c "SELECT version, description, applied_at FROM schema_migrations ORDER BY version;"

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# PostgreSQL Migration Runner
set -euo pipefail

DB="mydb"
USER="postgres"
MIGRATION_DIR="./migrations"

echo "=== Ensuring migrations table exists ==="
psql -U "$USER" -d "$DB" -c "
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS schema_migrations (
  version VARCHAR(14) PRIMARY KEY,
  applied_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
  description TEXT
);
"

echo ""
echo "=== Checking pending migrations ==="
for FILE in "$MIGRATION_DIR"/*.sql; do
  [ -f "$FILE" ] || continue
  VERSION=$(basename "$FILE" | cut -d_ -f1)
  DESCRIPTION=$(basename "$FILE" .sql | sed 's/^[0-9]*_//')

  APPLIED=$(psql -U "$USER" -d "$DB" -tAc "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM schema_migrations WHERE version = '$VERSION';")

  if [ "$APPLIED" = "0" ]; then
    echo "Applying: $FILE"
    psql -U "$USER" -d "$DB" -v ON_ERROR_STOP=1 --single-transaction -f "$FILE"
    psql -U "$USER" -d "$DB" -c "INSERT INTO schema_migrations (version, description) VALUES ('$VERSION', '$DESCRIPTION');"
    echo "Applied: $VERSION ($DESCRIPTION)"
  else
    echo "Skipping (already applied): $VERSION"
  fi
done

echo ""
echo "=== Migration history ==="
psql -U "$USER" -d "$DB" -c "SELECT version, description, applied_at FROM schema_migrations ORDER BY version;"

faq.question: Should I use a migration tool instead of raw SQL?
faq.answer: For larger teams, tools like Flyway, Liquibase, or golang-migrate add safety features like checksums and rollback tracking. For small projects or learning, raw SQL migrations with a tracking table work well.

faq.question: How do I handle rollbacks?
faq.answer: Create a corresponding down migration file for each up migration (e.g., 20260311_create_users_down.sql with DROP TABLE users). Apply the down file to revert. Always test rollbacks in staging first.
